title: VIDEO: Bitcoin Becomes an Election Issue in Canada — Pierre Poilievre on ETFs, Mining & Regulation
post_content:
Why Bitcoin entered Canada’s campaign conversation

Bitcoin has moved from a niche asset class into a topic politicians can’t ignore. In Canada, questions about inflation, household affordability, and trust in institutions have created fertile ground for “sound money” narratives. That’s why interviews and clips featuring Conservative MP Pierre Poilievre discussing Bitcoin and related policy ideas resonated widely online.

The debate is not only about price speculation. Voters increasingly ask what a government should do about custody, taxation, market integrity, and whether regulated products like spot or futures-based ETFs are an acceptable bridge for mainstream adoption. The more visible Bitcoin becomes, the more pressure regulators face to explain how they balance innovation with consumer protection.

ETFs, custody, and the “not your keys” trade‑off

When public figures say they hold Bitcoin exposure through an exchange-traded fund, it immediately triggers a familiar argument: self-custody versus convenience. ETFs can offer simpler reporting, easier access through brokerage accounts, and sometimes tax-advantaged wrappers. But they also centralize custody and introduce counterparty risk, which contradicts Bitcoin’s core “self-sovereign” ethos.

For a campaign, this nuance matters. A candidate can signal openness to crypto while still operating within the traditional financial system. The practical takeaway for voters is to separate the asset (BTC) from the wrapper (ETF) and to understand that regulation often grows around wrappers first because they are easier to supervise than private wallets.

Mining, regulation, and what could actually change

Beyond investment products, Bitcoin policy questions in Canada often touch energy and mining. Policymakers may frame mining as either a grid stabilizer that can absorb excess power or as an industry that competes with households for electricity. Any credible platform needs to address permitting, disclosure, environmental reporting, and how Canada wants to position itself in a global race for data centers and compute.

In practice, the most realistic near-term shifts are incremental: clearer taxation guidance, consistent rules for exchanges and custodians, and transparent standards for disclosures and advertising. The political “Bitcoin moment” is less about adopting BTC as legal tender and more about whether Canada aims to be a predictable jurisdiction where responsible crypto businesses can operate.

seo.meta_title: Bitcoin in Canada Election: Poilievre on ETFs & Mining
seo.meta_description: Watch how Bitcoin became a Canadian election topic: Pierre Poilievre discusses Bitcoin ETFs, mining, regulation, and what policy changes may follow.

title: Bank of England Tests Ripple (XRP) for Cross‑Border Payments
post_content:
What the Bank of England tested with Ripple technology

Cross-border payments are still slower and more expensive than the internet era suggests they should be. A typical international transfer can involve multiple intermediaries, time-zone delays, and reconciliation steps that stretch settlement from minutes into days. That friction is exactly what motivated the Bank of England to explore proof-of-concept work involving Ripple’s Interledger Protocol (ILP) in a simulated real-time gross settlement (RTGS) environment.

The goal of such tests is not to “pick a coin,” but to understand whether messaging, settlement finality, and synchronization between ledgers can be improved. For central banks, even a narrow experiment can surface practical issues: liquidity needs, operational risk, and how a shared “source of truth” might reduce manual reconciliation.

Where XRP fits in the conversation

Ripple’s ecosystem is often discussed alongside XRP because XRP can act as a bridge asset in certain payment flows. In enterprise narratives, the promise is simple: reduce the number of correspondent banking hops and move value with less capital trapped in pre-funded accounts. Critics, meanwhile, point out that outcomes depend on liquidity, market depth, and regulatory clarity across jurisdictions.

From a news standpoint, the Bank of England test is significant because it shows institutional interest in interoperability rather than a single-chain “winner.” ILP is designed to connect different networks, which aligns with how the real financial system works: many ledgers, many rules, and a constant need to translate value across them.

What to watch next for payments innovation

The hardest part of cross-border modernization is not the demo—it’s scaling. Wholesale payment scenarios raise different constraints than retail remittances: larger transaction sizes, tighter risk controls, and stricter compliance. Any future pilots will likely focus on liquidity management, resilience, and governance: who operates validators, how disputes are handled, and how institutions prove compliance without slowing everything down.

If additional proofs-of-concept follow, expect more emphasis on measurable benchmarks: settlement time, operational cost, error rates, and the ability to integrate with legacy systems. Whether or not XRP is used directly, experimentation around interoperability is a strong signal that the payments stack is being redesigned for a world where instant settlement is technically feasible.

seo.meta_title: Bank of England Tests Ripple XRP for Cross-Border Payments
seo.meta_description: Bank of England explored Ripple’s Interledger in a cross-border RTGS proof of concept. What it means for XRP, liquidity, and payments innovation.
